headline: Masks Work. Really. We'll Show You How.

beats.caption: Masks come in different types and materials, but they generally work the same way. Layers of fibers capture respiratory droplets and the smallest airborne particles — called aerosols — that can carry the coronavirus.
This process is called filtration, and a material's ability to capture particles is called its filtration efficiency.
Tightly woven cotton outperforms most common fabrics. A nonwoven material like that of an N95 respirator is the most effective of all.
Let's take a closer look at how filtration happens at the microscopic level.

Here you can see the difference in size between a mask's cotton fibers and aerosol particles of various diameters. The coronavirus is close in size to the smallest particles, but it usually travels inside the larger ones.
The fibers form a dense forest that particles carried on the airflow must find their way through. The laws of physics govern how fibers and particles interact — and how well the mask traps them.
The largest particles are the easiest to capture. They slam straight into the fibers and stick when the airflow carries them close, or when their momentum throws them off course.
The smallest particles bounce off air molecules along a random, zig-zagging path, which increases the time they spend in the fiber forest and the chance they'll be caught.
Medium-size particles are the hardest to filter. They escape capture because they follow the airflow and slip around the fibers.
These are the fibers of an N95 respirator. They are made of synthetic material, vary in size and are arranged at random.
They also have an extra advantage: an electrostatic charge that attracts and holds particles captive, whatever their size.
Where every other mask falls short, N95s excel. Their material filters at least 95% of the elusive medium-size particles — and even more of the large and small ones.
Fibers aren't the only factor in filtration. How well different masks filter also depends on their shape and fit.
Masks that fit loosely, or press against a beard or mustache, let aerosols leak through.
A good mask should be generous, snug at the edges, and shaped to leave space around the mouth and nose.
That creates a larger breathing zone to capture particles and increase the chance they meet the fibers.
Some masks have a valve that makes exhaling easier, but without a filter the valve lets aerosols through — so it doesn't protect others.
A well-fitted N95 is the best choice, but don't worry if you don't have one. When everyone wears a mask, the combined filtration adds up.
Say your mask filters half of the particles you exhale.
As they disperse into the air, the concentration of particles drops further.
The particles that do reach another person's mask are filtered again, reducing the number that get through.

“It's become clear that cloth masks, even if they aren't as effective as N95s, still help reduce transmission,” said Linsey Marr, an aerosol expert at Virginia Tech. “Even if you don't get to a 95% reduction, that's still better than nothing.”
Good ventilation and social distancing also lower the risk of transmission. And if everyone wears a mask and keeps their distance, the collective benefit grows.
